'''John Bentley''' (born 5 September 1966) is an English former [[rugby union]] and [[rugby league]] player who played in the 1980s and 1990s. Nicknamed 'Bentos', he was a [[List of dual-code rugby internationals|dual-code international]]. He won 4 caps in rugby union for [[England national rugby union team|England]] and toured with the [[British and Irish Lions|British Lions]] in 1997, winning 2 test caps, and in rugby league he won 5 international caps for [[England national rugby league team|England]], and 2 for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]].<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/>

He played club rugby union as a [[Rugby union positions#Wing|wing]] for [[Otley R.U.F.C.|Otley]], [[Sale Sharks|Sale]], [[Newcastle Falcons|Newcastle]] and [[Rotherham R.U.F.C.|Rotherham]]. In rugby league he played for [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]], [[Halifax R.L.F.C.|Halifax]] (two spells), [[Balmain Tigers]] and [[Huddersfield Giants]] as a {{rlp|wg}}. 

==Background==
Bentley was born in [[Dewsbury]], [[West Riding of Yorkshire]], England. He is an Honorary President of the Yorkshire region of the rugby charity [[Wooden Spoon Society]], and hosts and coaches at rugby union camps across England with Sale Sharks, Newcastle Falcons, and Leeds Carnegie.{{cn|date=August 2025}}

==Career==
Originally a rugby union player, he made his [[England national rugby union team|England]] début against [[Ireland national rugby union team|Ireland]] in 1988 aged 21 and played on the summer tour against [[Australia national rugby union team|Australia]].

After training as a police officer he switched codes from rugby union, which was then an amateur game, to rugby league, where he became a professional. He played for [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]],<ref>{{cite web |title=Leeds Rhinos Nostalgia: How John Bentley proved to be an exceptional code-breaker |url=https://www.yorkshireeveningpost.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/leeds-rhinos-nostalgia-how-john-bentley-proved-to-be-an-exceptional-code-breaker-3157365 |website=Yorkshire Evening Post |access-date=31 July 2025 |date=8 March 2021}}</ref> before moving on to [[Halifax R.L.F.C.|Halifax]], as well as enjoying a short spell in Australia with [[Balmain Tigers]]. He played for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]], and for England in the [[1995 Rugby League World Cup]]

When rugby union became openly professional in 1995, Bentley returned to the code, signing for [[Newcastle Falcons]] in 1996. He made 3 appearances as they won their first and so far only [[Guinness Premiership|Premiership]] title in 1998.<ref>{{cite news
 |url=http://stats.espnscrum.com/scrum/rugby/records/player/records.html?id=2529;team=249;type=tournament 
 |title=Allied Dunbar Premiership, 1997/98 / Newcastle Falcons / Player records 
 |publisher=espnscrum.com 
 |access-date=7 September 2014 
 |url-status=dead 
 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140907235203/http://stats.espnscrum.com/scrum/rugby/records/player/records.html?id=2529%3Bteam%3D249%3Btype%3Dtournament 
 |archive-date= 7 September 2014 
}}</ref> He was selected for the [[1997 British and Irish Lions tour to South Africa]], where he played in the second and third Tests. After the tour he played twice more for England at rugby union before switching back to rugby league with [[Huddersfield Giants]].

He was inducted into the Halifax Hall of Fame<ref>{{cite web|website=Halifax Panthers 'Hall of Fame'|title=John Bentley|url=https://halifaxpanthers.co.uk/the-club/hall-of-fame/}}</ref>
